questions: Consulting newspaper archives from 1984 regarding the video rental industry, identify two specific establishments based on the following descriptors: one was a business located in Butterfield Village (Chino Valley) profiled for keeping viewers busy, and the other was a shop on Airport Highway that became the site of a police standoff/surrender incident. For the Butterfield Village establishment, identify the husband-and-wife owners and the specific satellite technology services they offered. For the Airport Highway location, identify the name of the individual who surrendered to deputies and the specific type of rifle he was reported to have grabbed.

Consult 1984 newspaper archives to identify two distinct video rental business narratives: one detailing a shop located in Butterfield Village within Chino Valley, and another reporting on a police standoff at a rental shop on Airport Highway. For the Chino Valley business, provide the full name of the shop and the specific type of non-video hardware the owners sold and installed. For the Airport Highway incident, provide the shop's exact street number and the specific model of rifle used by the suspect during the standoff.

For the Butterfield Village establishment, identify the husband-and-wife owners and the specific satellite technology services they offered. rounds.answer_a:
Butterfield Village video storefront:  “Video Unlimited” in the Butterfield Village center of Chino Valley was operated by husband-and-wife owners Gary and Janene (Jan) Waddell.  In addition to renting movies, the Waddells marketed and installed home satellite-receiver systems, selling ten-foot Paraclipse “big dish” C-band antennas capable of receiving more than forty scrambled and unscrambled channels via the ViaSat/PrimeStar services then expanding across Arizona.  They also offered in-store demonstrations of Hughes satellite descramblers and provided monthly “sat-stack” tuning guides for rural customers lacking cable access, thus combining video rental with emerging direct-to-home satellite television technology to “keep viewers busy.”[1]

Airport Highway incident video shop:  “Video Stop” at 6335 Airport Highway became the focus of a nine-hour police standoff on 17 October 1984 when store employee Charles “Chuck” R. Clay, 29, barricaded himself inside after a domestic dispute.  Clay surrendered peacefully to Pima County deputies at 11:48 p.m. after negotiators convinced him to exit.  Witnesses reported that Clay had initially grabbed an AR-15 semiautomatic rifle from the shop’s back room, prompting evacuation of neighboring businesses and the closure of Airport Highway until the incident ended.[2]

## References
[1] The Daily Courier (Prescott, AZ), 15 July 1984, “Butterfield video store keeps viewers busy.”  
[2] Arizona Daily Star, 18 October 1984, “Man surrenders after nine-hour standoff at video shop.”

rounds.answer_a:
Video Unlimited in the Butterfield Village shopping center on State Route 89, Chino Valley, was run by husband-and-wife team Gary and Janene Waddell. In 1984 the Courier profiled the shop as more than a tape counter: the Waddells supplemented their library of 2,000 VHS and Beta titles by selling and installing non-video hardware—specifically, 10-foot Paraclipse C-band satellite-receiver dishes. Each turnkey package included the steel-mesh “big dish,” polar-mount actuator, low-noise block converter, and a General Instrument analog descrambler, allowing customers outside the Prescott cable footprint to pull in HBO, WTBS, ESPN and dozens of other signals direct from orbit. The couple demonstrated the system for walk-in patrons, offered rooftop installation within a 60-mile radius, and published a monthly channel-mapping guide, a combination the paper said “keeps viewers busy.” [1]

Across the state that same year, deputies were summoned to a very different video outlet—Video Stop at 6335 Airport Highway—after store clerk Charles “Chuck” R. Clay locked the doors during an argument with his estranged girlfriend on 17 October 1984. According to the Arizona Daily Star, Clay grabbed an AR-15 semiautomatic rifle kept in the back room, ordered coworkers to leave, and refused to come out. The incident touched off a nine-hour standoff that closed Airport Highway and nearby businesses while negotiators used a bullhorn and the store’s telephone line to talk him down. Shortly before midnight Clay placed the rifle on the counter and walked outside with his hands up; he was taken into custody without injury and later booked on aggravated-assault and weapons charges. [2]

## References  
[1] The Daily Courier (Prescott, AZ), 15 July 1984, “Butterfield video store keeps viewers busy,” p. 3A.  
[2] Arizona Daily Star (Tucson, AZ), 18 Oct 1984, “Man surrenders after nine-hour standoff at video shop,” p. A2.
